questions.questionText: ACE inhibitors (e.g., enalapril, ramipril) are first-line for heart failure because they:
questions.optionB: Reduce preload, afterload, and prevent cardiac remodeling by blocking angiotensin II formation
questions.explanation: ACEi: block conversion of Angiotensin I → Angiotensin II. Effects: decreased Ang II → vasodilation (reduced afterload), decreased aldosterone → natriuresis (reduced preload), decreased bradykinin degradation → vasodilation. Prevent cardiac remodeling (fibrosis). Increase survival in HF, post-MI, diabetic nephropathy. Side effects: dry cough (bradykinin), hyperkalemia, angioedema. Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic) and bilateral renal artery stenosis. Reference: KD Tripathi — ACE Inhibitors.

questions.optionB: Hypokalemia (K+ and digoxin compete for same binding site on Na+/K+ ATPase)
questions.explanation: Digoxin: cardiac glycoside, inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase → increased intracellular Na+ → increased Ca2+ (via Na+/Ca2+ exchanger) → positive inotropy. K+ and digoxin compete for same binding site → hypokalemia increases digoxin binding → toxicity. Also: hypercalcemia, hypomagnesemia potentiate toxicity. Symptoms: nausea/vomiting, visual disturbances (yellow-green halos), cardiac arrhythmias (PAT with block, bidirectional VT). Treatment toxicity: correct K+, digoxin-specific Fab antibodies (Digibind). Reference: KD Tripathi — Digoxin.

questions.optionB: Venodilation (decreases preload) and coronary vasodilation → reduces myocardial oxygen demand
questions.explanation: Nitroglycerin (GTN): releases NO → activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation. Primarily venodilation (decreases preload, reduces wall tension) > arteriolar dilation. Also dilates coronary arteries (especially epicardial) and relieves coronary spasm. Given sublingual (bypasses first-pass), IV, transdermal. Tolerance: occurs with continuous use → nitrate-free interval (10-12h overnight). Contraindicated with PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) → severe hypotension. Reference: KD Tripathi — Nitrates.

questions.questionText: Amiodarone is classified as a Class III antiarrhythmic and acts by:
questions.optionB: Blocking potassium channels (prolonging repolarization/APD) — also has Class I, II, IV properties
questions.explanation: Amiodarone: most effective antiarrhythmic, broadest spectrum. Primarily Class III (K+ channel blocker → prolongs APD and ERP). Also has: Class I (Na+ block), Class II (β-block), Class IV (Ca2+ block) properties. Uses: both supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias, refractory VT/VF. Long half-life (~40-55 days). Toxicities (many): pulmonary fibrosis (most serious), thyroid dysfunction (hyper/hypo — contains iodine), corneal microdeposits, hepatotoxicity, blue-gray skin, peripheral neuropathy, photosensitivity. Reference: KD Tripathi — Amiodarone.

questions.optionB: Competitively inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase → decreased hepatic cholesterol synthesis → upregulation of LDL receptors
questions.explanation: Statins: competitive inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting enzyme of cholesterol synthesis). Decreased hepatic cholesterol → upregulated LDL receptors → increased LDL clearance from blood → reduced LDL 30-50%. Also: increase HDL, decrease TG modestly, pleiotropic effects (anti-inflammatory, endothelial function, plaque stabilization). Most effective drugs for lowering LDL. Side effects: myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (rare), elevated liver enzymes. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Reference: KD Tripathi — Statins.

Warfarin: vitamin K antagonist → inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1) → decreased synthesis of factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C, S. Monitored by PT/INR (extrinsic pathway — factor VII has shortest half-life, first to be affected). Target INR: 2-3 (most indications), 2.5-3.5 (mechanical heart valves). Heparin: monitored by aPTT. Warfarin crosses placenta (teratogenic — nasal hypoplasia, chondrodysplasia). Antidote: vitamin K (slow), FFP/PCC (rapid). Reference: KD Tripathi — Warfarin.

questions.questionText: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet drug that acts by:
questions.optionB: Irreversibly blocking P2Y12 (ADP) receptor on platelets
questions.explanation: Clopidogrel: thienopyridine, irreversibly blocks ADP receptor (P2Y12) on platelets → inhibits ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation. Prodrug — requires CYP2C19 activation (polymorphism affects efficacy). Uses: ACS, post-PCI (with stent — dual antiplatelet: aspirin + clopidogrel), stroke prevention. Other P2Y12 inhibitors: prasugrel (more potent, more bleeding), ticagrelor (reversible, fastest onset). GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors: abciximab, eptifibatide, tirofiban. Reference: KD Tripathi — Clopidogrel.

questions.questionText: Amlodipine belongs to which class of antihypertensives?
questions.optionB: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (preferentially blocks vascular L-type Ca2+ channels → vasodilation)
questions.explanation: Amlodipine: dihydropyridine (DHP) CCB — selectively blocks vascular L-type Ca2+ channels → peripheral vasodilation → decreased BP. Long half-life (~30-50h). Does NOT significantly affect cardiac conduction (unlike non-DHP: verapamil, diltiazem). Uses: hypertension, stable angina, Raynaud's. Side effects: peripheral edema (ankle), reflex tachycardia, flushing, headache, gingival hyperplasia. Non-DHP (verapamil/diltiazem): negative chronotropic/dromotropic — used for rate control in AF. Reference: KD Tripathi — CCBs.
